title: Psalms 146
subtitle: Praise the LORD, My Soul

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Do Not Put Your Trust in Princes”

heb.paragraph: "Do not put your trust in princes (nədîbîm), in human beings, who cannot save. When their spirit departs, they return to the ground; on that very day their plans come to nothing" (vv.3–4). The first of five final hallelujah psalms. Princes die; God endures. "Blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob, whose hope is in the LORD their God" (v.5). The Maker of heaven and earth, sea and everything in them — he remains faithful forever (v.6).

notes.note: "Praise the LORD, my soul. I will praise the LORD all my life; I will sing praise to my God as long as I live." Lifelong praise: the commitment that opens the final five.

"Do not put your trust in princes. When their spirit departs, their plans come to nothing. Blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob." The contrast: mortal princes vs eternal God. Plans that die with the planner vs help that outlasts everything.

refs.note: Mary's Magnificat echoes these themes: God lifts the humble, scatters the proud.
Stop trusting in mere mortals, who have but a breath in their nostrils.
We are bringing you good news: turn from worthless things to the living God.

hist.context: Psalm 146 opens the Final Hallel, five psalms (146-150) each beginning and ending with 'Hallelujah' (Praise the LORD). This psalm contrasts trusting princes—whose breath departs and plans perish—with trusting YHWH, maker of heaven and earth. The living God helps the helpless; mortal rulers cannot save. This forms the theological foundation for the collection's cosmic praise.

heb.paragraph: "The LORD reigns (mālak̅) forever, your God, O Zion, for all generations. Praise the LORD" (v.10). What God does: upholds the cause of the oppressed (v.7), gives food to the hungry (v.7), sets prisoners free (v.7), gives sight to the blind (v.8), lifts up those bowed down (v.8), loves the righteous (v.8), watches over the foreigner (v.9), sustains the fatherless and the widow (v.9). Jesus reads Isaiah 61 in Nazareth and describes this exact ministry (Luke 4:18–19).

notes.note: "He upholds the oppressed, gives food to the hungry, sets prisoners free, gives sight to the blind, lifts up those bowed down, loves the righteous, watches over the foreigner, sustains the fatherless and widow." EIGHT divine actions for the vulnerable. Jesus’ mission statement (Luke 4:18–19) echoes this catalogue.
"The LORD reigns forever, your God, O Zion, for all generations. Praise the LORD." The hallelujah conclusion. The reign is forever; the praise is forever; the generations are forever.

refs.note: Jesus reads Isaiah: good news to the poor, freedom for prisoners, sight for the blind.
Jesus's messianic credentials: the blind receive sight, the lame walk.
The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord—he reigns forever.

hist.context: The catalog of divine actions reads like a messianic job description: executing justice for the oppressed, giving food to the hungry, setting prisoners free, giving sight to the blind, lifting up the bowed down, loving the righteous, watching over the foreigner, sustaining the fatherless and widow. Jesus cited these as evidence of his messianic identity. The LORD reigns forever—Zion's God across all generations.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: I will sing praise to my God as long as I live…

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: Blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWhen their spirit departs, they return to the ground; on that very day their plans come to nothing.ESVWhen his breath departs, he returns to the earth; on that very day his plans perish.NIVHe is the Maker of heaven and earth, the sea, and everything in them— he remains faithful forever.ESVwho made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them, who keeps faith forever;

rec.text: ""Praise the LORD, my soul. I will praise the LORD all my life; I will sing praise to my God as long as I live." Lifelong praise: the commitment that opens the final five."

rows.label: Princes Die — 146:1–5
rows.text: "Do not trust in PRINCES." Their plans die with them. "Blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob."

hebtext.note: "Do not put your trust in princes (nədîbîm), in human beings, who cannot save. When their spirit departs, they return to the ground; on that very day their plans come to nothing" (vv.3–4). The first of five final hallelujah psalms. Princes die; God endures. "Blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob, whose hope is in the LORD their God" (v.5). The Maker of heaven and earth, sea and everything in them — he remains faithful forever (v.6).

"The LORD reigns (mālak̅) forever, your God, O Zion, for all generations. Praise the LORD" (v.10). What God does: upholds the cause of the oppressed (v.7), gives food to the hungry (v.7), sets prisoners free (v.7), gives sight to the blind (v.8), lifts up those bowed down (v.8), loves the righteous (v.8), watches over the foreigner (v.9), sustains the fatherless and the widow (v.9). Jesus reads Isaiah 61 in Nazareth and describes this exact ministry (Luke 4:18–19).

themes.note: Mercy peaks: eight actions for the vulnerable. Justice: oppressed upheld, prisoners freed. Prophecy: Jesus’ Nazareth manifesto echoes this psalm. First of five hallelujahs.

prayer_prompt: Do not put your trust in princes, in human beings who cannot save. Consider asking God about the human deliverers you've trusted — and what disappointment has taught you.
